title: Understanding AI Capabilities
description: Go deeper into how AI models like Claude work — understanding context windows, tokens, temperature, and why AI sometimes gets things wrong.
learningObjectives: Explain how language models generate responses using pattern recognition and probability
Understand the concepts of context windows and tokens and how they affect your interactions with Claude
Identify why AI hallucinations happen and apply strategies to reduce their impact

sections.title: How Language Models Work (Simplified)
sections.content:
You do not need a PhD to understand how Claude works. The basic idea is more intuitive than you might think, and having a mental model of what is happening "under the hood" will make you a much more effective AI user.

**Predicting the next word.** At the most fundamental level, a language model like Claude works by predicting what word (or part of a word) should come next in a sequence. If you type "The capital of France is," the model recognizes a very strong pattern and predicts "Paris." This sounds simple, but when you scale this ability up to billions of patterns learned from an enormous amount of text, the results are remarkably sophisticated. Claude can hold conversations, write essays, solve math problems, and generate code — all through increasingly complex pattern prediction.

**Training is like reading every book ever written.** Before Claude can predict anything, it goes through a training process where it processes vast amounts of text data. During training, the model adjusts millions of internal settings (called "parameters") to get better at predicting what comes next. Think of it like a student who has read so many examples that they can anticipate patterns in new situations they have never seen before. The model does not memorize specific sentences — it learns the underlying patterns of language, reasoning, and knowledge.

**Beyond simple prediction.** Modern models like Claude go through additional training stages beyond basic pattern learning. Anthropic uses a process called RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback) where human trainers evaluate Claude's responses and help it learn which answers are more helpful, honest, and harmless. This is why Claude does not just predict random text — it tries to give you responses that are genuinely useful. Think of it as the difference between someone who can finish your sentences and someone who can finish your sentences in a way that actually helps you.

**Why this matters for you.** Understanding that Claude works through pattern recognition helps explain both its strengths and its limitations. It is great at tasks that follow recognizable patterns (writing, analysis, coding) and less reliable when it needs to go beyond its training (current events, niche expertise, novel reasoning). When Claude gives a confident-sounding but wrong answer, it is because the patterns pointed in the wrong direction — not because it is trying to deceive you.

adaptedExamples.getting-started: When Claude writes a polished email from your rough notes, it is drawing on patterns from millions of examples of good writing — not copying a template. Understanding this helps you see why it handles common writing tasks well but may stumble on highly specialized jargon it rarely encountered during training.

sections.title: Context Windows and Why They Matter
sections.content:
One of the most practical things to understand about Claude is the concept of a "context window." This directly affects how you interact with AI and how much you can accomplish in a single conversation.

**What is a context window?** The context window is the total amount of text that Claude can "see" at one time during a conversation. Think of it like Claude's working memory — everything in the conversation (your messages, Claude's responses, any documents you share) takes up space in this window. Once the window is full, older parts of the conversation start to fade from Claude's awareness.

**Tokens: how AI measures text.** AI models do not count words the way we do. Instead, they break text into smaller pieces called "tokens." A token is roughly three-quarters of a word in English. The word "understanding" is two tokens. A short sentence might be 10 tokens. A full page of text is around 300-400 tokens. Claude's context window can hold a very large number of tokens (often 100,000 or more), which means you can share substantial documents and have long conversations.

**Practical implications.** The context window affects your conversations in several important ways:

• **Long conversations lose early context.** If you have a very long back-and-forth, Claude may not remember details from the beginning of the conversation. If something important was discussed early on, you may need to remind Claude of it.
• **Document length matters.** You can share long documents with Claude for analysis, but extremely long texts may fill up a significant portion of the context window, leaving less room for conversation.
• **Each conversation starts fresh.** Claude does not remember previous conversations. Every time you start a new chat, it has no memory of what you discussed before. This is by design for privacy, but it means you may need to re-explain context when starting fresh.

**Tips for managing context effectively.** Keep your most important information at the beginning of your message. If a conversation is getting very long and Claude seems to be losing track, consider starting a new conversation with a summary of the key points. When sharing documents, consider including only the most relevant sections rather than pasting in entire files.

playground.description: See how the amount of context you provide changes the quality of Claude's response. Try the vague request first, then edit the user message to add specific details (what language, what level, what the code should do) and compare the results.
playground.systemPrompt: You are a helpful assistant. Answer the user's request as best you can given the information provided.

adaptedExamples.getting-started: If you paste a long report into a chat and then ask detailed questions twenty messages later, Claude may have lost track of the report's specifics. To get better answers, start a fresh conversation and re-paste the relevant section alongside your question so it stays inside Claude's working memory.

sections.title: Why AI Hallucinates (and What That Means)
sections.content:
"Hallucination" is a term you will hear a lot in AI discussions. Understanding why it happens is essential for using AI responsibly and effectively.

**What is an AI hallucination?** An AI hallucination occurs when Claude generates information that sounds plausible and is presented confidently, but is actually incorrect or entirely made up. For example, Claude might cite a research paper that does not exist, attribute a quote to the wrong person, or invent a historical event that never happened. The response reads convincingly, which makes hallucinations particularly tricky to spot.

**Why does this happen?** Remember that Claude works by predicting what text should come next based on patterns. Sometimes those patterns lead it astray. If you ask about a lesser-known topic, Claude's patterns might blend information from multiple sources in ways that produce something plausible but inaccurate. It is not lying — it genuinely does not "know" the difference between correct and incorrect information the way a human does. It produces what its patterns suggest is the most likely response.

**When hallucinations are most likely.** Hallucinations tend to occur more frequently in certain situations:

• **Specific facts, names, and numbers.** Claude might get a date, a statistic, or a person's name wrong, especially for less well-known subjects.
• **Citations and references.** If you ask Claude to cite sources, it may generate plausible-looking but fictional citations. Always verify any sources it provides.
• **Niche or specialized topics.** The less commonly a topic appeared in training data, the more likely Claude is to fill in gaps with plausible-sounding but inaccurate information.
• **When asked to be comprehensive.** If you push Claude to give you a definitive, exhaustive answer on something, it may stretch beyond what it actually knows reliably.

**What you can do about it.** Ask Claude to flag when it is uncertain. Verify important facts with reliable external sources. For critical tasks, ask Claude to explain its reasoning step by step — this makes errors easier to spot. And remember, hallucinations do not make Claude useless — they just mean you need to be a thoughtful, active user rather than a passive one.

playground.description: Test Claude with a question that is prone to hallucination. After you get a response, try editing the user message to ask Claude to rate its own confidence and flag anything it is unsure about. Notice how the response changes.
playground.systemPrompt: You are a helpful research assistant. Answer the user's question to the best of your ability.
playground.userMessage: Who won the 2017 Thurber Prize for American Humor, and what book did they win it for? Give me a brief summary of the book.

adaptedExamples.getting-started: If you ask Claude to recommend a book and it confidently names a title that sounds perfect, search for that title online before buying it — AI can invent plausible-sounding book names that do not actually exist. A quick verification step protects you from acting on hallucinated information.

sections.title: The Role of Training Data
sections.content:
Everything Claude knows comes from the data it was trained on. Understanding this helps you predict when Claude will be knowledgeable and when it might fall short.

**What is training data?** Training data is the massive collection of text that Claude learned from during its development. This includes books, articles, websites, academic papers, code repositories, and many other text sources. The breadth of this data is why Claude can discuss topics ranging from ancient history to modern programming languages.

**Knowledge cutoff.** Claude's training data has a cutoff date, which means it does not know about events, discoveries, or changes that happened after that point. This is different from a search engine, which can find information published today. When you ask about recent events, Claude may have outdated information or may not know about the topic at all. It is good practice to ask Claude when its knowledge was last updated and to verify any time-sensitive information.

**Biases and gaps in training data.** No dataset is perfectly balanced. Claude's training data likely contains more English-language content than other languages, more content about some countries and cultures than others, and more coverage of popular topics than niche ones. This means Claude may be more knowledgeable and nuanced about well-covered topics and less reliable on underrepresented ones. Being aware of this helps you know when to seek additional perspectives.

**Training data is not the same as memory.** Claude does not have a searchable database of its training data. It cannot tell you "I read this in a specific article on page 47." Instead, the training data shaped the patterns in its neural network. When Claude responds to you, it is drawing on these learned patterns rather than looking up specific documents. This is why it can synthesize and discuss ideas fluently but sometimes struggles with precise citations.

**Why this matters practically.** When you are using Claude, keep in mind that it will be strongest on topics that were well-represented in its training data — major world events, established scientific concepts, popular programming languages, well-known books and theories. It may be less reliable on very recent developments, highly specialized fields, local or regional information, and topics that are not widely discussed in English-language text.

adaptedExamples.getting-started: Claude can explain popular topics like major historical events or widely-used software in great detail, but it may give shallow or outdated answers about a small local festival or a niche hobby forum. When your question falls outside mainstream coverage, double-check with a specialized source.

sections.title: Test Claude's Capabilities and Limitations
sections.content: Now let us put your understanding to the test by exploring what Claude handles well and where it stumbles.

exercise.prompt:
Design a mini-experiment to test Claude's capabilities and limitations. Come up with THREE specific questions or tasks you would give to Claude, where:

1. **One task plays to Claude's strengths** — something where you expect Claude to do an excellent job. Explain why this leverages what you learned about how language models work.

2. **One task targets a known limitation** — something where you expect Claude to struggle or make an error. Explain which specific limitation you are testing (hallucination, knowledge cutoff, context, etc.).

3. **One task is in a gray area** — something where Claude might do well or might not, and you are genuinely unsure. Explain what factors would determine whether the response is reliable.

For each task, write the specific question you would ask Claude and explain your reasoning for why you categorized it the way you did.
hints: For the strength test, think about tasks that involve pattern recognition, language processing, or widely-known knowledge
For the limitation test, consider asking about very recent events, requesting specific citations, or asking about highly specialized or local topics
For the gray area, think about tasks that partially overlap with Claude's training but might require precise or current details
Your reasoning is more important than the specific questions — show that you understand WHY certain tasks are easier or harder for language models
exercise.evaluationCriteria: The response should include three specific, well-chosen tasks with clear reasoning. The strength task should genuinely play to language model capabilities (e.g., writing, analysis, explanation of well-known topics) with reasoning that references pattern recognition or broad training data coverage. The limitation task should target a real weakness (hallucination-prone scenarios, knowledge cutoff issues, or precision requirements) with an explanation of the underlying mechanism. The gray area task should show nuanced thinking about when AI might or might not be reliable, with reasoning that considers factors like topic coverage, specificity required, and verifiability. Deduct points if all three questions are trivially easy, if the reasoning lacks connection to how language models work, or if the categories do not match the actual tasks chosen.

sections.title: Check Your Understanding
sections.content: Reflect on what you have learned about how AI models like Claude work.

exercise.prompt: Explain in your own words: If Claude gives you a confident, detailed answer about a specific historical event, why should you still verify that answer? Connect your explanation to what you learned about how language models generate text.
exercise.evaluationCriteria: The response should demonstrate understanding that Claude generates text through pattern prediction rather than fact retrieval, meaning confident-sounding text does not guarantee accuracy. A strong answer will mention that hallucinations occur because the model produces what patterns suggest is likely rather than looking up verified facts. The response should also mention at least one practical verification strategy (checking with a trusted external source, asking Claude to explain its confidence level, or cross-referencing with authoritative references).

sections.title: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
Here is what you should carry forward about how AI works under the hood:

• **Language models predict text based on patterns.** Claude does not "know" things — it recognizes patterns from training and generates responses that follow those patterns. This is why it is powerful at language tasks and less reliable for precise facts.

• **Context windows are your working memory.** Everything in your conversation shares a finite space. Long conversations may lose early context, and each new chat starts completely fresh. Manage this by keeping key information prominent and summarizing when needed.

• **Hallucinations are a feature of the technology, not a bug you can fully avoid.** They happen because pattern prediction sometimes goes wrong. Be especially careful with specific names, dates, citations, and niche topics.

• **Training data shapes everything.** Claude's knowledge comes from its training data, which has a cutoff date and natural biases. It will be strongest on well-covered topics and less reliable on recent, local, or underrepresented subjects.

• **Understanding the mechanics makes you a better user.** You do not need to know every technical detail, but knowing the basics of how language models work helps you anticipate when to trust Claude and when to verify.
